content.text: The settings page requires an `AUX_PRESENTATION` entry for every registry id (it throws otherwise), and seeding is optional (the resolver falls back to `defaultRouteId` when unset). Let me read the presentation shape:

content.content: 84 const AUX_PRESENTATION: Record< 85 string, 86 { 87 subtitle: string; 88 description: React.ReactNode; 89 settingDescription: string; 90 effortSettingDescription?: string; 91 } 92 > = { 93 vision: { 94 subtitle: 'Reads uploaded images (diagrams, screenshots, photos)', 95 description: ( 96 <> 97 When an employee uploads an image (PNG, JPEG, WebP, or 98 HEIC), this model transcribes it to faithful text — verbatim text plus 99 the meaning of diagrams, tables, and layout — before claim extraction 100 runs over that text. The picker is filtered to{' '} 101 vision-capable models from any provider. The shipped 102 default is the Gemini extraction model; change it here with no redeploy. 103 104 ), 105 settingDescription: 'Vision model used to transcribe uploaded images to text before extraction.', 106 effortSettingDescription: 107 'Reasoning effort for image transcription. Most vision models ignore this; leave Off unless the chosen model benefits.', 108 }, 109 general: { 110 subtitle: 'Utility / fallback model for internal jobs', 111 description: ( 112 <> 113 Used for one-off internal tasks that don't fit the three stages 114 above — taxonomy cluster naming, on-demand classification, ad-hoc admin 115 queries. Pick whatever the current best general-purpose model is and 116 update it here when a newer one ships. The picker draws from the full 117 discovered catalog (no pool or stage requirements apply). 118 119 ), 120 settingDescription: 'General-purpose / utility model. Admin updates this when a newer model is available.', 121 }, 122 }; 123 124 const VISION_MODEL_BRIEF = `THE ORACLE — "Image Vision Model" role brief 125 (Paste this into a model-evaluation assistant or vendor comparison to judge whether a given model is the right pick for this setting.) 126 127 PURPOSE 128 This model performs Pass 1 of The Oracle's image-ingestion pipeline: it converts a single uploaded image into a faithful, self-contained PLAIN-TEXT rendering. It does NOT create knowledge itself — its text output is handed to a separate extraction model that mines structured "claims" from it. Its only job is high-fidelity image-to-text transcription and description. Everything downstream depends on this text being complete and accurate. 129 130 WHERE IT SITS IN THE PIPELINE 131 An employee uploads an image in a channel -> this model transcribes it to text -> the text is chunked, embedded, and stored as document_chunks -> a separate extraction model pulls operational claims, and each claim must quote this text exactly (deterministic quote-validation). If this model omits or hallucinates content, the knowledge base becomes silently wrong. Fidelity matters more than eloquence. 132 133 WHAT IT IS FED (input) 134 - Exactly ONE raw image per call, sent inline (PNG, JPEG, WebP, or HEIC). 135 - A fixed system instruction telling it to transcribe verbatim and describe visual meaning. 136 - A one-line request naming the file. 137 - No conversation history, no other documents, no tools. 138 Typical images: process diagrams, flowcharts, org charts, spreadsheets/tables, screenshots of apps or emails, photos of printed documents, whiteboards, shipping labels, handwritten notes, product photos. 139 140 WHAT IS EXPECTED (output) 141 - Plain text only — no markdown code fences, no "Here is..." preamble. 142 - VERBATIM transcription of every piece of visible text: labels, headings, every table cell, captions, callouts, stamps, axis labels, legends, handwriting. 143 - Diagrams/flowcharts/org charts rendered as an explicit TEXT TOPOLOGY — nodes as [Shape/Color: "verbatim label"], edges as annotated directional arrows ([A] --(condition)--> [B]), swimlanes/columns as headers — NOT free-form prose. Tables rendered row-by-row. Verbatim labels kept inside the nodes so they stay exactly quotable. 144 - Concrete operational content surfaced: rules, steps, routing, ownership, dates, quantities, statuses, conditions, exceptions. 145 - Honesty about uncertainty: explicitly flag anything unreadable or ambiguous; never invent detail. 146 147 PROCESS IT USES 148 A single, stateless, one-shot vision call. No caching (each image is unique), no tool calls, no multi-turn, no structured/JSON output. Just: see the image, emit faithful text. 149 150 WHAT MAKES A MODEL PERFECTLY SUITED 151 - Excellent OCR-grade text extraction, including dense tables and messy or handwritten text. 152 - Strong document and diagram understanding — reads charts, flowcharts, forms, and layouts, not just loose text. 153 - High instruction-following discipline: verbatim fidelity, no embellishment, no hallucination, and willingness to say "unreadable." 154 - Preserves reading order and table structure when rendering to prose. 155 - Generous max OUTPUT length, enough to fully render dense, text-heavy images. 156 - Low cost and reasonable latency: this runs once per uploaded image at volume and is asynchronous (no human waiting), so throughput and cost matter more than sub-second speed. 157 - High reliability and availability. 158 - Multilingual text recognition if the business handles non-English documents. 159 160 CAPABILITIES IT NEEDS (hard requirements) 161 - Image input (vision) — non-negotiable. 162 - Plain-text generation with strong instruction adherence. 163 - Sufficient max output tokens to transcribe a full page of dense content. 164 165 CAPABILITIES IT DOES NOT NEED (do not pay for these; some actively hurt) 166 - Tool / function calling — unused. 167 - Structured outputs / JSON schema / response_format — output is prose, not schema. 168 - Extended "thinking" / reasoning budget — transcription is perception, not deliberation; reasoning mostly adds latency and cost with no fidelity gain. Keep reasoning effort Off or Low. 169 - Large context window — input is one image plus a short prompt, nowhere near long-context territory. 170 - Streaming — output is consumed by a background worker, not rendered live. 171 - PDF input — PDFs are handled by a separate parsing path, not this model. 172 - Audio / video — out of scope. 173 174 BOTTOM LINE 175 Pick the cheapest, most reliable vision model with the best OCR plus document/diagram comprehension and the strictest instruction-following. Ignore reasoning, tools, structured output, and long context — they are irrelevant here, and reasoning effort should stay low.`; 176 177 const INTERVIEW_MODEL_BRIEF = `THE ORACLE — "Interview Model" role brief 178 (Paste this into a model-evaluation assistant or vendor comparison to judge whether a given model is the right pick for this setting.) 179 180 PURPOSE 181 This model is The Oracle's employee-facing conversation engine. It answers direct chat messages, asks clarifying questions, and helps employees surface operational knowledge without pretending the knowledge graph contains facts it does not contain. It is the human interface to the system, so tone, latency, groundedness, and restraint all matter. 182 183 WHERE IT SITS IN THE PIPELINE 184 An employee sends a chat message -> the app deterministically retrieves recent conversation, open gaps, approved claims, Brain snippets, and relevant evidence -> this model writes the assistant reply -> the reply and usage metadata are stored for audit. It does NOT perform live retrieval through model-side tools; retrieval happens before the model call and is injected into the prompt. It should use the supplied evidence, ask follow-up questions when knowledge is thin, and avoid making unsupported business assertions. 185 186 WHAT IT IS FED (input) 187 - The Oracle system prompt and conversation rules. 188 - Recent channel messages and the user's latest message. 189 - Deterministically retrieved approved claims, quotes, open gaps, Brain snippets, and metadata when relevant. 190 - Optional image/document attachment text or image parts when the selected route supports vision. 191 - Provider cache hints and, for Qwen, a reusable session handle. 192 Typical topics: process questions, shipment routing, product-development decisions, customer exceptions, designer file practices, system workflows, meeting follow-ups, and "is this still true?" recertification questions. 193 194 WHAT IS EXPECTED (output) 195 - A helpful, grounded chat response to the employee. 196 - Usually one concise answer or one high-value follow-up question. 197 - No invented policy, no fake citations, no claim IDs exposed to the employee unless the UI explicitly asks for them. 198 - Clear distinction between "the approved knowledge says..." and "I do not have enough evidence yet." 199 - Warm, tactful language for people-sensitive or blame-prone topics. 200 - Good behavior in multi-turn context: remember the current conversation, but do not treat recent chat as approved truth unless the prompt labels it that way. 201 202 PROCESS IT USES 203 A synchronous, user-facing text or multimodal generation call. The user is waiting. The model receives prebuilt context blocks and must compose a response directly. It may receive tool-oriented prompt structure, but the direct provider adapters do not run arbitrary Vercel AI SDK tools; choose a model for instruction following over agentic tool autonomy. 204 205 WHAT MAKES A MODEL PERFECTLY SUITED 206 - Low latency under real chat pressure, ideally comfortably under 8 seconds including retrieval and provider overhead. 207 - Excellent instruction following and refusal discipline: grounded answers, one-question behavior, no unsupported business facts. 208 - Strong long-context handling for channel history, retrieved evidence, and document/image attachment context. 209 - Good vision support for product photos, diagrams, screenshots, and uploaded-document images shared in chat. 210 - Tool/function-call capability and structured-output capability are useful because this role shares infrastructure with direct adapters and may be extended with more structured chat behaviors. 211 - Strong conversational tone: clear, calm, tactful, and natural with employees. 212 - Good cache behavior for reusable system and conversation prefixes. 213 - Reasonable cost: this runs on demand whenever employees chat, so cost can climb quickly.

content.text:
Done — the dedicated translation-route setting is implemented and pushed to PR [#1](https://github.com/u2giants/theoracle/pull/1) (commit `7f831c8`).

I added it the idiomatic way — as an **auxiliary model** in the registry (the repo's mechanism for models that aren't one of the 3 pipeline roles), so the picker, resolver, and models API pick it up with no new branches:

- **`default_translation_route`** setting key + `TRANSLATION_AUXILIARY_MODEL` registry entry, with **no capability filter** so you can pick any catalog model — including **Qwen** — for translation.
- **Admin → Settings → "Translation model"** card now renders (with copy explaining it translates approved claims, evidence/Brain stay original-language, pick a Chinese-native model for Mandarin quality).
- The **worker** now resolves via `resolveAuxiliaryRouteFromSettings('translation')`, falling back to the shipped default (multilingual Sonnet) when unset — so it works out of the box and you switch to Qwen whenever you like, no redeploy.

**Verification:** the `verify:auxiliary-defaults` guard passes (it confirmed the new entry's default route resolves to a live catalog route), and ai/workers/web all typecheck clean.

So the model routing is now fully admin-controlled per task: extraction, interview, vision, general, **and translation** are each independently selectable in the UI.

## Where the whole feature stands
- **Done & verified:** schema + migration, locale-aware retrieval (+ parity guard), `source_lang` stamping, claim-translation worker, chat-route localization, Brain-stays-English, drift documented, translation-route setting.
- **Remaining (not started):** the **backfill script** for existing approved claims, and optional **admin side-by-side translation review**.
- **Operational:** the migration still hasn't been applied — `pnpm db:migrate` (with `ORACLE_RUN_VECTOR_INDEXES=1` for the ANN index) is the next step when you're ready, then set a test employee's `locale` to `zh-CN` and pick Qwen as the translation model.

Want me to write the backfill script next, or pause here?

content.text:
Your catalog is populated live from DashScope/DeepSeek's `/models` endpoints, so the exact tiers your account exposes are whatever those return — there's no hardcoded list in the repo to enumerate. Let me answer on the merits and then handle the scope change, which needs a code adjustment.

## Which model for translation

First, a reality check: I can't authoritatively benchmark version-specific names like "qwen 3.7 max" or "deepseek v4" — some are at or past my knowledge cutoff, and they're dashboard labels that vary. So judge by **tier and task fit**, not my memory of a leaderboard.

The task is narrow and forgiving: translate a 1–3 sentence approved-claim summary EN↔ZH, faithfully, no reasoning, no vision, no tools. And it's **async + low-volume** (now even lower — only claims you select), so **latency and cost barely matter**. That points hard in one direction: **pick the highest-quality tier, not a "flash" tier.**

- **Qwen-Max (top Qwen tier) — my default recommendation.** Qwen is Alibaba's Chinese-native family; for EN↔ZH localization it tends to produce the most idiomatic, natural Mandarin register, which is exactly what matters for employees *reading* claims. The "max" tier maximizes fidelity, and since this is background work the higher per-token cost is negligible.
- **DeepSeek "pro" — fine second choice / fallback.** DeepSeek's strength is reasoning/coding; that's wasted on short-text translation. Capable in Chinese, but not where I'd start for *translation register*.
- **Skip the "flash" tiers** (qwen flash, deepseek flash) — they optimize the two things you don't care about (speed, cost) and trade away the one you do (quality).
- **Skip the "4v"/vision variant** — translation is text-only; vision adds nothing.

**The decisive test is empirical, and you've now got the tooling for it:** the translation-route setting I just added lets you swap models with no redeploy. Translate ~15 real claims (pick ones heavy on operational jargon, proper nouns, and numbers) with **Qwen-Max vs DeepSeek-pro**, and have a bilingual reviewer eyeball both. That A/B will settle it better than any ranking I could quote.

## Scope change — this needs a code adjustment

Right now the worker auto-translates **every** claim on approval. Your new rule ("only the ones I direct to the China team") means that auto-trigger is wrong and should come out. The good news: the design already supports selective translation cleanly —

**A claim shows in Chinese only if it has a translation row.** Retrieval does `COALESCE(translation, canonical)`, so a China-team reader automatically sees the Chinese rendering for translated claims and falls back to English for everything else. So "directed to the China team" = "you triggered translation for it" — no extra flag/column needed.

The change is just: **remove the translate-on-approval trigger, and add an explicit per-claim "Translate for China team" admin action** (a button on the claim) that fires the worker for that one claim. Existing/production claims are never touched.

I should confirm one UX detail before wiring it:

questions.question: How do you want to mark a claim for China-team translation?

options.label: Per-claim button
options.description: A "Translate for China team" button on each claim in the admin claims list. You click it on the specific claims you want translated. Simplest, most direct.

options.label: Checkbox + bulk action
options.description: Select multiple claims with checkboxes, then one "Translate selected for China" button. Better if you'll direct many claims at once.

options.label: Re-translate too
options.description: Same as per-claim button, but also re-show the button after edits so you can refresh a translation when a claim's summary changes.

input.new_string:
### 5.2 New worker: `claim-translation.ts` (`apps/workers/src/trigger/`)

**Translation is opt-in per claim** — not every approved claim is shared with the
China team. An admin selects claims in the Admin → Claims list (checkboxes) and
clicks "Translate selected for China team" (`translateClaimsForChina` action),
which triggers this worker for each selected claim. There is **no** automatic
on-approval translation, and existing/production claims are never auto-translated.
A claim is "directed to the China team" precisely when it has a `claim_translations`
row; retrieval's `COALESCE(translation, canonical)` then shows it in Chinese to the
China group and in English to everyone else (untranslated claims stay English for
all readers). The worker is idempotent and skips non-approved claims.

content.text:
Both things are handled. Here's the summary.

## Model recommendation (your question)

For translating short claim summaries EN↔ZH — async, low-volume, quality-sensitive — **pick the top tier, not a flash tier, and lean Qwen-Max:**

- **Qwen-Max (top Qwen tier) — my pick.** Chinese-native family, best idiomatic Mandarin register for employees *reading* claims; cost/latency are irrelevant here since it's background work on only the claims you select.
- **DeepSeek-"pro" — solid fallback**, but its reasoning strength is wasted on translation.
- **Skip the "flash" tiers** (qwen flash / deepseek flash) — they optimize speed+cost, which you don't care about, at the expense of quality.
- **Skip the "4v"/vision variant** — translation is text-only.

I can't authoritatively rank version-specific labels like "3.7 max" vs "v4 pro" (some are at/past my cutoff). The decisive move: **A/B Qwen-Max vs DeepSeek-pro on ~15 real claims** (jargon-heavy, with proper nouns + numbers) using the new translation-route setting, and have a bilingual reviewer judge. Cheap, and it settles it definitively.

## Scope change — implemented & pushed (commit `b0682da`)

You were right that auto-translating every approved claim was wrong. Changed to **opt-in per claim**:

- **Removed** the translate-on-approval auto-trigger. Existing/production claims are never touched.
- **Admin → Claims** now has a checkbox (🇨🇳 column) on **approved** rows + a **"Translate selected for China team"** bulk button. You tick the claims you're directing to the China team and submit.
- A claim is "directed to China" exactly when it has a translation row — retrieval's `COALESCE(translation, canonical)` already shows only those in Chinese to the China group; everything else stays English for everyone.
- Used the HTML `form=` attribute so the checkboxes attach to the bulk form without nesting the per-row approve/reject forms. Web typechecks clean.

So the feature is now: you flip a China employee's `locale` to `zh-CN`, set the translation model to Qwen (Admin → Settings → "Translation model"), then tick whichever claims that team should see in Chinese. Nothing else gets translated.

Still outstanding (and now mostly moot): the bulk **backfill script** is no longer needed given opt-in selection. The only operational step left is applying the migration (`pnpm db:migrate`) when you're ready. Want me to do anything else, or is this a good stopping point?

message.content:
when i refresh a page, do i still see which claims i routed and to whom?
does the checkbox + bulk route also work for english / non-translate claims?

content.text:
Great questions — they expose a real gap in what I built. Short answers:

1. **No** — as built, the checkboxes are transient. On refresh you'd see nothing indicating which claims you already routed. The "directed to China" signal *is* persisted (the presence of a `claim_translations` row), but the UI doesn't surface it. That's a usability hole I should fix.
2. The bulk action works on any **approved** claim. For an English-sourced claim, ticking it produces the Chinese rendering (the normal case). But there's a conceptual point worth clarifying — there's no separate "route without translate."

Let me fix #1 by surfacing persisted translation status, then I'll explain #2 precisely.

content.text:
Both addressed (commit `b3456d3`).

## 1. Persisted routing status — fixed

On refresh you now **do** see which claims you routed and to whom. The claims list queries `claim_translations` per claim and shows a green **✓ zh-CN** badge on every routed claim, with a tooltip "visible to China team (中文) in their language." This survives refresh because it reflects the actual translation rows in the DB, not transient checkbox state. The checkbox stays for "translate now"; the badge is the persisted record.

With only one non-English locale today, "to whom" is always the China team — but the badge is per-language, so if you ever add `zh-TW`, French, etc., each routed group shows its own badge automatically.

## 2. Does it work for English / non-translate claims?

Two things bundled here, so let me separate them:

**Does ticking an English claim work?** Yes — that's the main path. An English-sourced claim → the worker produces the Chinese rendering. (A Chinese-sourced claim would instead get an English rendering. The worker computes "translate into every supported language *except* the source language.")

**Is there a "route to China but keep it English"?** No — and importantly, you don't need one, because of how the one-brain model works:

> **All approved claims are already visible to every employee, including the China team.** There is no per-team visibility gate. The checkbox doesn't *grant access* to a claim — the China team can already retrieve every approved claim. The only thing the checkbox does is **add a Chinese rendering** so they read it in Chinese instead of English.

So:
- A claim you **don't** tick → still visible to the China team, just **in English**.
- A claim you **do** tick → visible to the China team **in Chinese**.

That means "route a non-translate / English claim to China" isn't a separate action — it already happens for free. You only tick the claims where the *Chinese wording* matters enough to spend a translation on.

One thing to flag so it's not a surprise: I scoped the checkboxes to **approved** claims (the worker skips non-approved anyway, since only approved claims surface in retrieval). If you ever want to "pre-route" a pending claim, tell me and I'll widen it — but routing an unapproved claim has no reader-facing effect until it's approved.

If your mental model actually wanted a true *visibility assignment* (China team sees only a curated subset, not the whole approved corpus), that's a different and bigger design — it would partition the "one brain" — so I held off. Say the word if that's what you intended and we can talk through it.

const TRANSLATION_MODEL_BRIEF = `THE ORACLE — "Translation Model" role brief
(Paste this into a model-evaluation assistant or vendor comparison to judge whether a given model is the right pick for this setting.)

PURPOSE
This model translates an approved business "claim" — a single short, factual operational statement — from its source language into another language, so employees can read the company's knowledge in their own language. The Oracle keeps ONE unified knowledge graph: a claim is authored once (its canonical text + supporting evidence stay in the original language) and this model produces a faithful rendering in the other language for display only. It does NOT create, interpret, summarize, or expand knowledge — it only re-expresses an existing claim in another language with zero drift.

WHERE IT SITS IN THE PIPELINE
A claim is extracted and approved -> an admin selects specific claims to direct to a language group (today: the China team, Simplified Chinese) -> the claim-translation background worker calls this model once per claim per target language -> the translated text is embedded (same embedding model as the canonical claim) and stored in claim_translations -> at chat time, retrieval shows each reader the rendering in their locale (COALESCE: translation if present, else the canonical text). The evidence quote and the synthesized "Brain" are NEVER translated — only the claim summary. So this model sits strictly on the read-side display path; nothing downstream re-derives meaning from its output except a human reading it and a semantic embedding for retrieval.

WHAT IT IS FED (input)
- Exactly ONE claim summary per call — typically one to three sentences of concise operational business prose (a rule, a routing decision, an ownership fact, a deadline, a status, an exception).
- A fixed system instruction telling it to translate faithfully into a named target language and output only the translation.
- The target language name.
- No conversation history, no other claims, no documents, no images, no tools.
Typical content: product-development steps, licensing/approval rules, shipment routing, customer-ops exceptions, designer file practices, ERP/PLM workflows. Expect proper nouns (people, products, systems, customers), internal codes, numbers, dates, and units.

WHAT IS EXPECTED (output)
- ONLY the translated text — no preamble, no quotes, no notes, no transliteration of the source.
- Exact preservation of meaning. No additions, omissions, softening, hedging, or editorializing — a mistranslated operational rule silently misleads the people who act on it.
- Natural, idiomatic register in the TARGET language — read like a native operations writer wrote it, not a literal word-for-word gloss. This matters most: employees read these to do their jobs.
- Faithful handling of proper nouns, product/system names, and codes — kept as-is unless a well-established target-language form exists; never invented.
- Numbers, dates, units, and quantities preserved exactly.
- Honest, conservative behavior on ambiguity — do not "improve" or guess; translate what is there.

PROCESS IT USES
A single, stateless, one-shot text-generation call per claim per language. Fully asynchronous — a background worker runs it; no human is waiting. Volume is LOW and bounded (only the specific claims an admin directs to a language group, not the whole corpus), and it is idempotent (re-run only when the source claim changes). There is no caching benefit (each claim is unique), no multi-turn, no tool use, and no structured/JSON output — the output is plain prose.

WHAT MAKES A MODEL PERFECTLY SUITED
- Best-in-class translation QUALITY for the specific language pair in use (here: English <-> Simplified Chinese), with idiomatic, natural target-language phrasing — not stilted literalism.
- For Chinese specifically, a Chinese-native model family (e.g. Qwen) typically has the strongest Mandarin register and handling of business idiom; weigh that heavily.
- High instruction-following discipline: outputs ONLY the translation, preserves proper nouns/numbers, never adds commentary.
- Faithfulness over fluency-at-any-cost: never drops or invents operational detail.
- Good terminology consistency on domain jargon and named entities.
- Because the job is async, low-volume, and not user-facing, COST and LATENCY barely matter — do not trade translation fidelity to save either. Prefer the higher-quality tier (e.g. a "max"/flagship tier) over a "flash"/cheap tier.
- High reliability/availability is nice but not critical (the worker is idempotent and retryable).

CAPABILITIES IT NEEDS (hard requirements)
- Excellent bilingual generation for the exact language pair in use.
- Strong instruction adherence for "translate only, output nothing else."
- Enough max output to cover a few sentences (tiny — never an issue).
- Modest context window — input is one short claim plus a short prompt; long-context is irrelevant.

CAPABILITIES IT DOES NOT NEED (do not pay for these; some actively hurt)
- Vision / image input — text only.
- Tool / function calling — unused.
- Structured outputs / JSON schema / response_format — output is plain prose.
- Extended "thinking" / reasoning budget — translation of a short statement is not deliberation; reasoning mostly adds latency and cost and can encourage over-editing. Keep reasoning effort Off or Low.
- Long context window — one short claim per call.
- Streaming — consumed by a background worker, not rendered live.
- Batch API discount — volume is low and selective; not worth the added plumbing.
- PDF / audio / video — out of scope.

BOTTOM LINE
Pick the model with the best, most natural translation quality for your exact language pair and the strictest "output only the translation" discipline — a Chinese-native flagship (e.g. Qwen-Max-tier) is a strong default for English<->Chinese. Ignore reasoning, tools, structured output, vision, and long context; keep reasoning effort low. Since it runs async on only the claims you direct to a language group, spend for fidelity, not speed or cost. Validate the final choice with a small A/B (this model vs one alternative) on ~15 real claims judged by a bilingual reviewer.`;

// Clipboard "job brief" text by auxiliary-model id. Declared after the brief
// literals (which are large) so the earlier AUX_PRESENTATION map stays free of
// forward references. Aux models without a brief simply omit an entry.
const AUX_CLIPBOARD_BRIEFS: Record<string, string> = {
  vision: VISION_MODEL_BRIEF,
  translation: TRANSLATION_MODEL_BRIEF,
};

content.text:
#2 is done and pushed (commit `5925562`). The **Translation model** card on Admin → Settings now shows the same "copy job brief" button as the other roles — it copies a detailed, paste-ready brief covering the job (faithful EN↔ZH claim-summary translation, display-only, evidence never translated), the capabilities that matter (target-language fidelity + strict "output only the translation" discipline; cost/latency irrelevant since it's async + low-volume), and the ones to *not* pay for (vision, tools, JSON, reasoning, long context). Drop it into any model-comparison tool and it'll have what it needs.

## On #1 — "bulk route" → "bulk ask someone"

Thanks for the correction; I want to get this right before building. To be clear on what's already in place vs. what you're describing:

- What I built is **translate selected claims** for the China team — a *read-side* feature (they read existing approved knowledge in Chinese). That still stands and is independent.
- "Bulk **ask someone**" sounds like a *write-side* feature: posing questions to people to *get* knowledge back — which is a different flow (the Oracle's "gaps"/interview side), not translation.

I don't want to guess at the shape, since it changes what I build and I'd need to look at how questions currently reach employees first.

questions.question: What should bulk 'ask someone' do? (This is a write-side / question-asking feature, separate from claim translation.)

options.label: Ask a group a question

content.text: Good — "verify selected claims" (bulk recertification: send claims to people to confirm/correct). That's a real write-side feature, and I shouldn't build it blind. I need to understand how the Oracle currently asks employees questions and whether any verification/recertification machinery already exists. Let me map that first.

input.description: Map question-asking & verification
input.prompt:
I'm scoping a new feature for The Oracle (a Next.js 16 + Trigger.dev + Drizzle/Postgres monorepo at D:\repos\oracle): an admin bulk action to "send selected claims to people to verify/confirm/correct (recertify — 'is this still true?')". Before designing it, I need to understand the existing machinery. Do a thorough read-only investigation and report findings with file:line references and concise conclusions. Do NOT edit anything.

Answer these specific questions:

1. GAPS: Where is the `gaps` table defined (packages/db/src/schema.ts) — full column list? How are gaps created, surfaced, and resolved? Is a gap's `questionToAsk` ever shown/posted to employees, and through what surface (injected into chat prompt? a dedicated UI page? Teams? notifications?)? Look at retrieval.ts (getRelevantOpenGaps), the chat route, any admin gaps page, and any gap-generation worker.

2. ASKING EMPLOYEES: How does the Oracle pose a question TO an employee today? Is there any code path that inserts a message into a channel programmatically, or posts to Teams, or otherwise prompts a specific person/group to answer something? Search for message insertion, channel creation, Teams posting, notifications. Is there any "direct message"/DM concept or a way to start a conversation with a chosen employee?

3. VERIFICATION / RECERTIFICATION: Is there ANY existing concept of claim verification, recertification, "is this still true", staleness, or claim expiry? Check claim_metadata (effective_from/effective_until), claims.status enum values, and grep for "verif", "recertif", "stale", "confirm", "expire". Does anything already let a human confirm/refute an existing claim?

4. GROUPS / TARGETING: How are employees grouped or targeted? Is there any team/group concept beyond `employees.departments` (text[]) and the new `employees.locale`? How would I express "the China team" or "these 3 people" as a target? Look at channels, channel_participants, employees.

5. ADMIN ACTION + WORKER PATTERNS: Briefly, the conventional pattern for (a) an admin server action that triggers background work (apps/web/app/admin/**/_actions.ts + apps/web/lib/trigger.ts), and (b) a Trigger.dev worker that posts/creates something. One concrete example of each is enough.

Report structure: one short section per numbered question, each with the key file:line refs and a 1-3 sentence conclusion. End with a 3-5 bullet synthesis of what building blocks already exist vs. what would have to be net-new for a "bulk verify selected claims by asking people" feature.

content.text: I have a clear map now. The existing **`gaps`** table is the natural vehicle — it already has `gapType`, `relatedClaimIds`, `questionToAsk`, `whyItMatters`, `targetEmployeeId`/`targetDepartment`, a `status` lifecycle, and an existing path that posts gap questions into employees' chat. So "verify selected claims" = create recertification gaps for the selected claims and let the existing ask-in-chat machinery surface them. Let me confirm one schema detail (is `gapType` an enum or free text?) before I propose the design:

export const gaps = pgTable(
  'gaps',
  {
    id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
    gapType: varchar('gap_type', { length: 50 }).notNull(),
    sectionId: varchar('section_id', { length: 255 }).references(
      () => brainSections.id,
    ),
    relatedClaimIds: jsonb('related_claim_ids'),
    relatedContradictionId: uuid('related_contradiction_id').references(
      () => contradictions.id,
    ),
    questionToAsk: text('question_to_ask').notNull(),
    whyItMatters: text('why_it_matters').notNull(),
    targetEmployeeId: uuid('target_employee_id').references(() => employees.id),
    targetDepartment: varchar('target_department', { length: 255 }),
    priority: gapPriorityEnum('priority').notNull(),
    status: gapStatusEnum('status').default('open').notNull(),
    askedInMessageId: uuid('asked_in_message_id').references(() => messages.id),
    resolvedByClaimId: uuid('resolved_by_claim_id').references(() => claims.id),
    createdByModelRunId: uuid('created_by_model_run_id').references(() => modelRuns.id),
    createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow().notNull(),
    updatedAt: timestamp('updated_at').defaultNow().notNull(),
    resolvedAt: timestamp('resolved_at'),
  },
  (t) => ({
    statusPriorityIdx: index('gaps_status_priority_idx').on(t.status, t.priority),
    targetStatusIdx: index('gaps_target_status_idx').on(t.targetEmployeeId, t.status),
  }),
);

export const oracleInterventions = pgTable('oracle_interventions', {
  id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
  channelId: uuid('channel_id')
    .references(() => channels.id)
    .notNull(),
  triggerType: oracleInterventionTriggerTypeEnum('trigger_type').notNull(),
  relatedGapId: uuid('related_gap_id').references(() => gaps.id),
  relatedContradictionId: uuid('related_contradiction_id').references(
    () => contradictions.id,

content.text:
`gapType` is free `varchar(50)`, not an enum — so this whole feature reuses the existing `gaps` table with **zero schema change**. Here's the design I'd build.

## Proposed MVP: "Verify selected claims"

Reuse the gaps machinery end-to-end:

1. **Admin UI** (claims list): a second bulk form alongside "Translate for China team" — tick approved claims, pick a target, click **"Ask to verify."**
2. **Server action** → new `claim-recertification` worker (fire-and-forget, mirroring the translation one).
3. **Worker**: for each selected claim, draft an "is this still accurate?" question via `OracleAIClient` (quoting the claim summary + why it matters), then insert a **gap** row: `gapType='claim_recertification'`, `relatedClaimIds=[claimId]`, `questionToAsk`, `whyItMatters`, target set, `status='queued'`, `priority`. If the target is a `zh-CN` employee, draft the question **in Chinese** (nice tie-in with the locale work).
4. **Surfacing** uses the existing path — `getRelevantOpenGaps()` injects the recertification question into that person's next Oracle chat (and the lull-interjection worker can post it into an active channel). No new notification infra.
5. **Resolution**: the person's reply flows through normal extraction (becomes evidence / a new or contradicting claim); admin reviews and marks the gap resolved. Existing `gaps` admin page already supports resolve/stale/reject.
6. **Status visibility**: like the translation badges, show a "🔁 asked" badge on claims with an open recertification gap, so refresh shows what you've already sent and to whom.

This is almost entirely reuse — no schema migration, no new tables, no new notification system. The one genuine fork is **who gets asked**, since it shapes the target picker and how the gap is addressed:

questions.question: When you bulk 'ask someone to verify' selected claims, who is the target?

options.label: A specific employee I pick
options.description: Per batch, choose one employee from a dropdown; all selected claims' verify questions go to them (gaps.targetEmployeeId). Most literal 'ask someone'.

options.label: The claim's original author
options.description: Each claim is sent back to whoever asserted it ("you said this — still true?"). Per-claim target, no picker needed; natural for recertification.

options.label: A department / the China team
options.description: Pick a group (a department, or the China-locale group); the question surfaces to everyone in it (gaps.targetDepartment). Needs a small targeting tweak for the locale group.

options.label: Let me pick employee OR group
options.description: Support both: a target picker offering individual employees and groups. More UI, most flexible.

content.text:
Both items are done and pushed.

## 1. Model-chooser brief button (commit `5925562`)
The **Translation model** card in Admin → Settings now has the same "copy job brief" button as the other roles. It copies a detailed, paste-ready spec — purpose (faithful EN↔ZH claim-summary translation, display-only, evidence never translated), the capabilities that matter most (target-language fidelity + strict "output only the translation" discipline; cost/latency irrelevant because it's async + low-volume), and the capabilities to *not* pay for (vision, tools, JSON, reasoning, long context). Hand it to any model-comparison tool and it has what it needs.

## 2. Bulk "ask to verify" — built (commit `d83a291`)
This is the feature you actually meant by "bulk route." On the claims list, the same checkboxes now drive **two** actions (via separate submit buttons):

- **Translate selected for China team** (existing), and
- **Ask [target] to verify** — pick the **China team** or an **individual employee**, and it asks them to confirm/correct each selected claim ("is this still accurate?").

It reuses the existing `gaps` machinery — **no schema change**. The worker drafts a short recertification question in the target's language (Chinese for the China team), records a gap per claim/target, and the question reaches people through the same gap→chat path the Oracle already uses. Their replies flow back through normal extraction; you resolve the request in the existing `/admin/gaps` page. A **🔁 verify** badge marks claims with a pending ask, so — like the translation ✓ badges — **it survives refresh** and answers "who did I already ask."

Two scoping notes:
- I limited targets to **China team + individual employees**. I dropped per-department targeting because gap department-matching compares against employees' free-text `departments`, which may not equal the departments-table ids — I didn't want to ship a subtly-broken target. The worker keeps a department branch internally so we can enable it once that matching is verified.
- Verification is **opt-in/manual** (you pick claims and a target), and the question surfaces through the existing gap mechanism rather than a new push/notification system — consistent with how the Oracle already asks people things.

All of `ai`/`workers`/`web` typecheck clean. As before, none of this is live until the migration is applied (`pnpm db:migrate`) — though note this particular feature needs no new migration; it's the earlier bilingual schema that does.

content.text:
Confirmed: a "group" = a department (`departments` enum + `employee_departments` junction). The clean way to satisfy "translate only for China recipients" is to **fan every target out to individual employees and create one gap per recipient, drafted in that recipient's language** — so China employees get a Chinese question, everyone else English, and a mixed group does the right thing per-member. That also sidesteps the department-name matching issue (gaps target `employeeId`, not a department string).

Let me rewrite the worker to take multiple targets and resolve them to recipients:

content.text:
Done and pushed (commit `617dff4`).

## What changed

**Multi-target asks.** The "ask to verify" picker is now a **multi-select** — you can combine, in one send:
- the **China team** (zh-CN locale group),
- one or more **departments** (groups), and
- individual **employees** (Ctrl/Cmd-click for multiple).

**Conditional translation, exactly as you specified.** The worker fans every target out to concrete recipient employees and creates **one gap per recipient, drafted in that recipient's own language**. The consequence:

- Sent to a China employee → question in **Chinese**.
- Sent to an English employee → **English**.
- Sent to a **group containing China employees** → the China members get Chinese, the rest get English.
- An **all-English** target → no Chinese draft is ever produced (nothing wasted).

So "translate only if it goes to a China employee or a group containing China employees" falls out naturally — translation happens per-recipient, never blanket. Drafts are cached per language (a 7-person mixed department = one English draft + one Chinese draft → 7 gaps), and it stays idempotent per (claim, employee).

**Groups are reliable now.** Because targets resolve to `employeeId` via the `employee_departments` junction (enum-keyed, exact), department targeting no longer risks the free-text name-matching problem — so I re-enabled it.

## One honesty note on scope

I built this on the **"ask to verify selected claims"** action (which is what creates gaps in this flow). Your phrasing — "send gaps (ask someone)" — reads as a general capability, and this delivers the targeting + translation behavior for the claim-verification path. If you also want a **free-form "compose a question and send it to these targets"** surface (a gap not tied to a specific claim), or a **"send to targets" action on the existing `/admin/gaps` page** for the auto-generated gaps, those are separate surfaces I haven't built — say the word and I'll add them using this same recipient-resolution + per-language primitive.

As always: none of this is live until `pnpm db:migrate` runs (the recertification feature itself needs no new migration; it reuses the `gaps` table — it's the earlier bilingual schema that does).

content.text:
You asked me to run the production migration — I have your explicit go-ahead, and it's the sanctioned path (`pnpm db:migrate`). The change is additive and low-risk: a new `claim_translations` table plus two `DEFAULT 'en' NOT NULL` columns (constant-default = metadata-only in Postgres, no table rewrite) and an idempotent FTS index. I'll skip the optional HNSW vector index (it's wasteful on an empty table; can add later with `ORACLE_RUN_VECTOR_INDEXES=1`).

Let me check there's nothing uncommitted first, then run it.

content.text:
All done.

## Migration — applied & verified ✅
`pnpm db:migrate` ran successfully against the **theoracle** production database (`vokucjpanhvqunimlvsp`). Confirmed live:
- `claim_translations` table created
- `claims.source_lang` + `employees.locale` columns added (default `'en'`, so existing rows backfilled safely)
- the `lang` + two FTS indexes created
- HNSW vector index correctly skipped (build it later with `ORACLE_RUN_VECTOR_INDEXES=1` once there's translation data)

The `NOTICE ... already exists, skipping` lines during the run were just the idempotent hand-SQL files (10–66) re-running as designed — no errors.

## Commit & push — nothing to do
Everything was already committed and pushed across the earlier commits. `git db:migrate` only writes to the database (and Drizzle's tracking table inside it), not to any local files, so the working tree is **clean** and the branch is **up to date with origin** at `617dff4`. The only untracked files are the unrelated `rfq-*.png` screenshots, which I've left alone.

So there was no new commit to make — the code that *describes* this migration (schema, `0007_tricky_charles_xavier.sql`, the FTS index file) was already on the branch and on GitHub.

The China bilingual feature is now **fully live in the database**. To start using it: set a China employee's `locale` to `zh-CN`, pick a translation model at Admin → Settings → "Translation model" (Qwen, per the brief), then use the claims list to translate or "ask to verify" the claims you direct to them.

content:
AI TASK SPEC: Repository Documentation Maintenance
ROLE
You are an AI coding agent updating Markdown documentation for a software repository.
Your documentation must help future developers and future AI coding sessions understand the actual current state of the repository without relying on prior chat context.
Documentation is part of the work. It is not optional and not a final cleanup step.
________________________________________
PRIMARY OBJECTIVE
Make AGENTS.md the canonical operating guide and documentation router for future developers and AI coding sessions.
AGENTS.md MUST let a new senior engineer or AI session understand the repository in under 5 minutes.
AGENTS.md MUST prevent future AI sessions from ingesting every .md file unnecessarily.
________________________________________
ABSOLUTE RULES
•	MUST derive documentation from the actual repository state.
•	MUST inspect relevant code, config, scripts, workflows, migrations, deployment files, and existing docs before editing docs.
•	MUST NOT invent architecture, intent, future plans, deployment behavior, credentials, identifiers, business rules, or undocumented conventions.
•	MUST NOT document guesses as facts.
•	MUST mark unknowns clearly.

| Add or change configuration, env vars, feature flags, secrets, or runtime settings | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/configuration.md`, `docs/deployment.md` if prod/runtime env is affected | Unrelated architecture docs |
| Change local setup, dev scripts, test/lint/debug workflow, package scripts, or tooling | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/development.md`, relevant package/config files | `docs/deployment.md` unless CI/CD changes |

Use this format:
## Container and service inventory

•	node_modules/

Looks like:
[How a new developer or AI session might misread it.]

[What it really is.]

[Constraint, incident, requirement, or tradeoff.]

## Credentials and environment

...

Impact:
...

...

...

...

|---|---|---|

CLAUDE.md MUST stay short.
CLAUDE.md MUST NOT duplicate AGENTS.md.
CLAUDE.md MAY include only:
•	Read AGENTS.md first

•	.claudeignore guidance
•	allowed operations/tools/APIs
•	SSH permissions, if any
•	explicit note that SSH is not the normal deployment path unless actually true
•	commit style preferences
•	Claude-specific behaviors to enable or suppress
If guidance applies to all AI agents or all developers, put it in AGENTS.md, not CLAUDE.md.
________________________________________

MUST create or update these files when appropriate:
•	.claudeignore
•	.cursorignore
MUST use matching content unless there is a tool-specific reason not to.

dist/
node_modules/
.cache/
coverage/
MUST add generated, third-party, vendor, build, or cache directories that should not consume AI context.
MUST create .copilotignore if the repository uses GitHub Copilot and ignore rules matter there.
Ignore files MUST align with the “What to ignore” section of AGENTS.md.
________________________________________
HANDOFF RULE
IF any work is unfinished, blocked, or in progress:
•	MUST create HANDOFF.md in repo root.
•	MUST make it comprehensive enough for a new developer or AI session to continue without prior chat context.
•	MUST mention HANDOFF.md in AGENTS.md as required reading for continuation work.
HANDOFF.md MUST include:
•	what was being built or fixed and why
•	what is fully done
•	what is partially done and exact current state
•	future plans discussed during the session
•	what has not been started
•	decisions made and why
•	dead ends or abandoned approaches
•	exact next action
•	known risks, blockers, or unknowns
•	session context that would otherwise be lost
IF the work described in HANDOFF.md is complete:
•	MUST delete HANDOFF.md.
________________________________________
REQUIRED AI WORKFLOW
Follow this sequence:
1.	Inspect repository structure.
2.	Identify owned code, generated code, vendor/framework code, docs, scripts, migrations, deployment files, and build artifacts.
3.	Inspect existing Markdown docs.
4.	Determine which docs are stale, missing, redundant, or misleading.
5.	Update AGENTS.md first.
6.	Update supporting docs only where topic-specific detail belongs.
7.	Update README.md only for quick orientation.
8.	Update CLAUDE.md only for Claude-specific notes.
9.	Update folder-level README.md files only where local folder context is useful.
10.	Update .claudeignore, .cursorignore, and .copilotignore if applicable.
11.	Create, update, or delete HANDOFF.md according to the handoff rule.
12.	Verify documentation against actual repository files.
13.	Check that no secrets were added.
14.	Commit and push documentation changes to GitHub.
15.	Provide final completion report.
________________________________________
VERIFICATION GATES
Before final response, verify:
•	AGENTS.md exists.
•	AGENTS.md contains the required sections.
•	AGENTS.md includes the documentation map.
•	Documentation map routes tasks to docs instead of telling agents to read everything.
•	CLAUDE.md does not duplicate AGENTS.md.
•	Ignore files match documented ignore guidance where appropriate.
•	Env vars are listed without secret values.
•	Deployment docs describe actual deployment, not guesses.
•	HANDOFF.md exists if and only if work is unfinished, blocked, or in progress.
•	No secrets were written.
•	Stale docs were removed, corrected, or clearly marked.
________________________________________
FINAL COMPLETION REPORT
After committing and pushing, final response MUST include:
## Documentation changes

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `path/to/file.md` | Summary of change |

## Ignore files

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `.claudeignore` | Summary of change |

## Handoff status

- `HANDOFF.md`: present/absent
- Reason: ...

## Verification

- Docs verified against actual repo: yes/no
- Secrets added: yes/no
- Unknowns remaining: ...

## Remaining documentation gaps

- ...
If no documentation changes were required, final response MUST say so and explain why.

content.content:
586	### ESLint config imports eslint-config-next natively, not via FlatCompat
587	
588	Looks like:
589	`apps/web/eslint.config.mjs` imports `eslint-config-next/core-web-vitals` and spreads it directly, instead of the `FlatCompat`-based pattern `create-next-app` generates.
590	
591	Actually:
592	With `eslint-config-next` v16 + ESLint 9, the `FlatCompat` path throws `TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON` (the bundled react config has a circular `configs` object). v16 ships a native flat-config array, so importing it directly is the working path. The old `.eslintrc.json` + `next lint` were removed (Next 16 dropped the `next lint` subcommand).
593	
594	Do not change because:
595	Reverting to `FlatCompat` reintroduces the circular-structure crash; reverting to `next lint` breaks entirely (`next lint` no longer exists in Next 16).
596	
597	### Quote validation is fuzzy for transcripts, strict for documents
598	
599	Looks like:
600	`validateQuote` has an `allowFuzzy` path that accepts a quote when its tokens merely *overlap* the source — contradicting the "deterministic verbatim provenance / no fuzzy match" principle in `docs/oracle/03` and the comment at the top of `quote-validator.ts`.
601	
602	Actually:
603	Spoken Teams transcripts are disfluent and the extraction model paraphrases them, so the polished claim quote never appears verbatim in any utterance — strict matching rejected ~every transcript-derived claim. The fuzzy path (opt-in, enabled only on the message/transcript path in `claim-extraction.ts`; documents stay strict) is a **deterministic** token-overlap check (no LLM grader) and anchors the stored evidence to the **real** utterance text, not the model's paraphrase. See `DECISIONS.md` D-transcript-fuzzy-quote.
604	
605	Why:
606	Without it the entire Teams-transcript feature produces zero promotable claims. Provenance is preserved as "this real utterance supports this claim" rather than "the model copied these exact words."
607	
608	Do not change because:
609	Reverting to strict-only re-breaks transcript extraction. If tightening is wanted, raise `fuzzyMinOverlap` or restrict `allowFuzzy` to transcript-sourced messages — don't remove it.
610	
611	### `raw_transcripts` is hand-written SQL, not in schema.ts
612	
613	Looks like:
614	`raw_transcripts` (the original VTT per call) is missing from `packages/db/src/schema.ts`, and the ingestion worker reads/writes it with raw `sql` instead of Drizzle.
615	
616	Actually:
617	It's defined only in the hand-written `packages/db/migrations/sql/62_raw_transcripts.sql` (idempotent `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`), like the observability views. Keeping it out of `schema.ts` avoids a drizzle-kit drift entry for an ancillary raw-storage table the typed query layer never needs.
618	
619	Why:
620	The VTT is stored so the whole pipeline stays re-runnable from true source after Microsoft expires the transcript (`messages` are a lossy transform). See `DECISIONS.md` D-raw-transcripts.
621	
622	Do not change because:
623	Adding it to `schema.ts` would make drizzle-kit want to generate a migration for an already-applied hand-written table (drift). Leave it as hand-written SQL.
624	
625	### The Oracle MCP server is lazy-loaded on purpose — `tools/list` stays at five tools
626	
627	Looks like:
628	`apps/web/lib/mcp/capabilities.ts` defines real operations (search claims, list domains, read Brain sections) but none are registered as MCP tools. Only five generic tools appear in `tools/list` (`health`, `list_capabilities`, `tool_search`, `get_capability_details`, `invoke_tool`). It looks like indirection that could be simplified by registering each operation directly as its own MCP tool.
629	
630	Actually:
631	This is a deliberate lazy-loaded capability registry. Real operations live in a hidden registry and are reached via `invoke_tool` after discovery through `tool_search` / `get_capability_details`. Keeping `tools/list` tiny means MCP clients that cache the initial list never miss capabilities — we intentionally do **not** rely on `tools/list_changed`. The `verify:mcp` guard (`apps/web/lib/mcp/__verify__/mcp-registry.ts`, wired into the Vercel build gate + `pr-check.yml`) asserts `tools/list` is exactly those five.
632	
633	Why:
634	The endpoint is for external AI agents building software for us; the design follows the house lazy-registry standard (same shape as the `devops-mcp` / `synology-monitor` servers). It is read-only and only surfaces approved knowledge (`searchWithRetrievalPlan` filters `status = 'approved'`; Brain tools require `review_status = 'approved'`).
635	
636	Do not change because:
637	- Registering capabilities directly as MCP tools reintroduces the exact anti-pattern the design avoids and breaks `verify:mcp` (build fails).
638	- The live endpoint is `/api/mcp/mcp`. The doubled segment is correct, not a typo: `mcp-handler` requires a `[transport]` route segment and `basePath: '/api/mcp'` derives the Streamable-HTTP endpoint as `<basePath>/mcp`.
639	- New write (tier-2+) capabilities MUST go through the `invoke_tool` preview/confirm gate; never let a write run without `args.confirmed === true`.
640	- Full design + "how to add a capability" lives in `apps/web/lib/mcp/README.md` — read it before changing the MCP surface.
641	
642	### Uploaded images become knowledge via a two-pass vision→text→extract flow
643	
644	Looks like:
645	`document-ingestion` could just send an uploaded image straight to a vision model and ask it for claims. Instead it runs a vision model first to produce a text transcription, then runs the normal extraction model over that text.
646	
647	Actually:
648	This is deliberate and load-bearing for provenance. Pass 1 (`transcribeImageToText`) renders the image to faithful text (a structured text topology for diagrams — nodes/edges/swimlane headers, verbatim labels kept inside the nodes). Pass 2 is the unchanged chunk → extract → quote-validate → promote pipeline. Every claim's `exactQuote` is validated against a `document_chunk`, and an image has no text to validate against — so the transcription IS the chunk text, and the verbatim-label rule keeps quotes matchable. The transcription is persisted, so the whole thing stays re-runnable and auditable.
649	
650	Why:
651	A single-pass "image → claims" call would force bypassing quote validation, breaking the candidate-before-claim provenance guarantee. Keep the two passes separate.
652	
653	Do not change because:
654	Inline image input is implemented only in the Vertex adapter (`toVertexParts` → Gemini `inlineData`); the worker formats the part per provider. The vision route is pinned via the auxiliary registry, not the extraction route, so flipping the extraction model to a non-vision provider does not silently break image ingestion.
655	
656	### Document extraction quotes must stay inside persisted chunks
657	
658	Looks like:
659	The extractor could read a whole uploaded document and return any true statement with a quote from anywhere in the document.
660	
661	Actually:
662	`document-ingestion` persists `document_chunks`, formats extraction input as labeled chunk blocks, and expects document-derived candidates to use the exact chunk id as `sourceMessageId`. The promotion executor validates every `exactQuote` against one persisted chunk. Existing uploaded rows keep their old chunks until re-uploaded or deliberately reprocessed with chunk recreation.
663	
664	Why:
665	Document claims need quote-level provenance that can be re-run and audited. A quote spanning two chunks, even if semantically correct, cannot be promoted because there is no single chunk evidence row that contains it verbatim.
666	
667	Do not change because:
668	Letting document extraction use document-level source ids, paraphrased quotes, or cross-chunk quotes breaks the candidate-before-claim evidence contract and can make valid-looking claims impossible to trace.
669	
670	Large uploads are processed by `buildDocumentChunkWindows()` in bounded extraction windows. There should be no document-level "first N characters only" cap; each window covers whole persisted chunks and produces its own extraction batch/model run. Reintroducing a silent whole-document cap will make long company docs appear to process successfully while losing most of the knowledge.
671	
672	### Markdown document quotes normalize formatting, not meaning
673	
674	Looks like:
675	A Markdown upload should require the model to reproduce every `**`, table pipe, heading marker, and link exactly.
676	
677	Actually:
678	Document quote validation uses `MARKDOWN_DOCUMENT_NORMALIZATION_POLICY` for text/markdown uploads. It deterministically strips or normalizes Markdown syntax such as emphasis markers, heading/list prefixes, inline code ticks, links/images to display text, and table separators before matching. It does not rewrite meaning, do fuzzy matching, or allow cross-chunk quotes.
679	
680	Why:
681	Business-process docs commonly contain tables and formatted labels. Models often quote the visible text instead of the raw Markdown punctuation, so strict raw matching rejected true claims even when the source chunk contained the visible statement.
682	
683	Do not change because:
684	Documents still need deterministic quote-level provenance. If a new format needs looser matching, add an explicit normalization policy for that format rather than enabling transcript-style fuzzy matching on documents.
685	
686	### Extraction picker intentionally does not require vision
687	
688	Looks like:
689	The Extraction stage handles uploaded-image knowledge, so the model picker should require vision.
690	
691	Actually:
692	Images are transcribed by the separate auxiliary Image Vision model before Extraction sees them. `claim-extraction` and the extraction pass inside `document-ingestion` call `OracleAIClient.runObject<ExtractionOutput>()` over text blocks and expect structured JSON. The hard picker requirements for Extraction are therefore structured output plus context window, not image input.
693	
694	Why:
695	Requiring vision on Extraction filters out strong text/JSON extraction models for a capability the runtime does not use. The provenance-critical image path remains protected by `default_vision_route`.
696	
697	Do not change because:
698	Adding `vision` back to `STAGE_REQUIREMENTS.extraction` in `apps/web/lib/stage-requirements.ts` should only happen if a runtime path starts sending raw image parts directly into the extraction route again. Verify with `document-ingestion.ts` first.
699	
700	### Auxiliary models are a registry, not a 4th pipeline role
701	
702	Looks like:
703	The image-vision model selection looks like it should be a 4th `OracleModelRole` next to interview/extraction/synthesis.
704	
705	Actually:
706	`OracleModelRole` is intentionally frozen at exactly 3 pipeline stages (each with a strict primary+fallback catalog pair, stage requirements, pools, batch dispatch). Vision and general-purpose are "auxiliary models" — single-pick selections with at most one capability filter and a default route — defined in `AUXILIARY_MODELS` (`packages/ai/src/routes/auxiliary.ts`) and resolved by `resolveAuxiliaryRouteFromSettings`. The settings page, picker, and `/api/admin/models` iterate the registry; none of them special-case `'vision'` or `'general'` by string.
707	
708	Why:
709	Auxiliary models have none of a pipeline role's structure, and folding them into `OracleModelRole` would ripple through every `Record<OracleModelRole, …>` map and the strict 1-primary/1-fallback invariant. The registry adds new utility-model selections with zero new branches.
710	
711	Do not change because:
712	Adding `'vision'` to `OracleModelRole` to "unify" things reintroduces exactly the ripple the registry avoids. Add a registry entry instead.
713
